speaker: BRUTUS
text: Come, poor remains of friends, rest on this rock.

speaker: CLITUS
text: Statilius showed the torchlight, but, my lord,

speaker: CLITUS
text: He came not back. He is or ta’en or slain.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Sit thee down, Clitus. Slaying is the word;

speaker: BRUTUS
text: It is a deed in fashion. Hark thee, Clitus.

speaker: CLITUS
text: What, I, my lord? No, not for all the world.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Peace, then, no words.

speaker: CLITUS
text: I’ll rather kill myself.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Hark thee, Dardanus.

speaker: DARDANUS
text: Shall I do such a deed?

speaker: CLITUS
text: O Dardanus!

speaker: DARDANUS
text: O Clitus!

speaker: CLITUS
text: What ill request did Brutus make to thee?

speaker: DARDANUS
text: To kill him, Clitus. Look, he meditates.

speaker: CLITUS
text: Now is that noble vessel full of grief,

speaker: CLITUS
text: That it runs over even at his eyes.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Come hither, good Volumnius. List a word.

speaker: VOLUMNIUS
text: What says my lord?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Why this, Volumnius:

speaker: BRUTUS
text: The ghost of Caesar hath appeared to me

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Two several times by night—at Sardis once

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And this last night here in Philippi fields.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I know my hour is come.

speaker: VOLUMNIUS
text: Not so, my lord.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Nay, I am sure it is, Volumnius.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Thou seest the world, Volumnius, how it goes.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Our enemies have beat us to the pit.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: It is more worthy to leap in ourselves

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Than tarry till they push us. Good Volumnius,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Thou know’st that we two went to school together;

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Even for that our love of old, I prithee,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Hold thou my sword hilts whilst I run on it.

speaker: VOLUMNIUS
text: That’s not an office for a friend, my lord.

speaker: CLITUS
text: Fly, fly, my lord! There is no tarrying here.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Farewell to you—and you—and you, Volumnius.—

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Strato, thou hast been all this while asleep.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Farewell to thee, too, Strato.—Countrymen,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: My heart doth joy that yet in all my life

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I found no man but he was true to me.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I shall have glory by this losing day

speaker: BRUTUS
text: More than Octavius and Mark Antony

speaker: BRUTUS
text: By this vile conquest shall attain unto.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: So fare you well at once, for Brutus’ tongue

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Hath almost ended his life’s history.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Night hangs upon mine eyes; my bones would rest,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That have but labored to attain this hour.

speaker: CLITUS
text: Fly, my lord, fly!

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Hence. I will follow.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I prithee, Strato, stay thou by thy lord.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Thou art a fellow of a good respect;

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Thy life hath had some smatch of honor in it.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Hold, then, my sword, and turn away thy face

speaker: BRUTUS
text: While I do run upon it. Wilt thou, Strato?

speaker: STRATO
text: Give me your hand first. Fare you well, my lord.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Farewell, good Strato.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Caesar, now be still.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: I killed not thee with half so good a will.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: What man is that?

speaker: MESSALA
text: My master’s man.—Strato, where is thy master?

speaker: STRATO
text: Free from the bondage you are in, Messala.

speaker: STRATO
text: The conquerors can but make a fire of him,

speaker: STRATO
text: For Brutus only overcame himself,

speaker: STRATO
text: And no man else hath honor by his death.

speaker: LUCILIUS
text: So Brutus should be found.—I thank thee, Brutus,

speaker: LUCILIUS
text: That thou hast proved Lucilius’ saying true.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: All that served Brutus, I will entertain them.—

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Fellow, wilt thou bestow thy time with me?

speaker: STRATO
text: Ay, if Messala will prefer me to you.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Do so, good Messala.

speaker: MESSALA
text: How died my master, Strato?

speaker: STRATO
text: I held the sword, and he did run on it.

speaker: MESSALA
text: Octavius, then take him to follow thee,

speaker: MESSALA
text: That did the latest service to my master.

speaker: ANTONY
text: This was the noblest Roman of them all.

speaker: ANTONY
text: All the conspirators save only he

speaker: ANTONY
text: Did that they did in envy of great Caesar.

speaker: ANTONY
text: He only in a general honest thought

speaker: ANTONY
text: And common good to all made one of them.

speaker: ANTONY
text: His life was gentle and the elements

speaker: ANTONY
text: So mixed in him that nature might stand up

speaker: ANTONY
text: And say to all the world “This was a man.”

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: According to his virtue, let us use him

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: With all respect and rites of burial.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Within my tent his bones tonight shall lie,

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Most like a soldier, ordered honorably.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: So call the field to rest, and let’s away

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: To part the glories of this happy day.
